Introduction

Module Overview

🚂
Welcome to Module 4

This module covers the securement of unattended railway equipment — one of the most safety-critical skills in railway operations. An unsecured train caused the Lac-Mégantic disaster that killed 47 people. This module ensures you never make that mistake.

Duration

Approximately 4 hours including knowledge checks and activities.

Rules Covered

CROR Rules 112, 66, and 109 — the three rules governing securement.

🔔

How to use this module: Read each page carefully. Every knowledge check must be completed before moving forward. Use the ← → keys or the Previous / Next buttons to navigate.

Introduction

Learning Objectives

By the end of this module, you will be able to:

  • Define "unattended"

    State the CROR definition and identify when it applies in the field.

  • Identify hand brake types

    Recognize and correctly operate all three types of hand brakes.

  • Test effectiveness

    Follow the four-step procedure to confirm hand brakes will hold.

  • Calculate hand brake requirements

    Use the CROR 112(g) chart with grade and trailing tons.

  • Apply emergency grade protocols

    Execute the correct sequence under Rule 66 for heavy and mountain grades.

  • Secure equipment in any location

    Apply the right method for main track, yard, and high-risk locations.

💡

The core idea: Equipment left unattended must be positively secured so it cannot move unintentionally — regardless of terrain, weather, or duration.

Section 1

The Night of July 6, 2013

47
people never came home
Lac-Mégantic, Québec  ·  July 6, 2013  ·  1:14 AM

A 73-car freight train loaded with crude oil rolled unmanned down a 1.2% grade for 11.6 kilometres, reaching 105 km/h before derailing in the centre of town. The resulting explosion and fire levelled the downtown core and killed 47 people.

⚠

This was not an act of nature or a technical failure. It was a preventable securement failure — the direct result of too few hand brakes applied to a very heavy train on a grade.

Section 1

The Train: Facts & Figures

Emergency responders at the disaster site. Photo: Wikimedia Commons / Transportation Safety Board of Canada
73
Tank cars of crude oil
10,290
Trailing tons
0.92%
Grade at parking location
6M L
Oil spilled & ignited
📋

The train had been parked on the main track at Nantes, QC by a single-person crew who then went to a hotel for mandatory rest. When the locomotive engines were shut down — partly due to a fire — air pressure bled off, and the hand brakes could not hold.

Section 1

What Went Wrong

Applied

7

hand brakes — set by the locomotive engineer before leaving the train unattended.

Required (TSB finding)

17–26

hand brakes — the minimum needed to hold 10,290 tons on a 0.92% grade.

The Transportation Safety Board identified 18 causes and contributing factors, but the mechanical root cause was simple: the hand brake count was fatally insufficient. The engineer had not consulted the CROR hand brake chart — or if he had, he miscalculated.

💡

The gap was 10–19 hand brakes. Learning to read the CROR 112(g) chart correctly is the single most important skill in this module.

Section 1

Video: The Documentary

Watch the following clip — then answer the discussion question below.

▶
Lac-Mégantic: This Is Not an Accident
CBC documentary series. Clips also available on CBC Gem and YouTube.
Search on YouTube ↗
💬

Discussion: Based on the video, what single action could have prevented the disaster? Who bears responsibility when equipment moves unintentionally?

Discuss with a classmate for 3 minutes, then move on.

📖

The full documentary series "Lac-Mégantic: This Is Not an Accident" (2023) is available on CBC Gem and investigates corporate and regulatory failures in depth. The TSB report R13D0054 is publicly available at tsb.gc.ca.

Section 1

Lessons That Changed Safety

Following Lac-Mégantic, Transport Canada issued emergency directives and overhauled CROR Rule 112. Key changes included:

  • Mandatory use of the CROR hand brake chart (trailing tons × grade)
  • Two-person confirmation of securement before leaving equipment
  • Prohibition of single-person crews on trains with dangerous goods on grades
  • Enhanced oversight of hand brake effectiveness testing
  • Revised Rule 66 protocols for emergency brake applications on heavy and mountain grades
✅

The rules in this module exist because of July 6, 2013. Every hand brake requirement, every confirmation procedure, every grade protocol — these were written in response to 47 deaths. They are not bureaucratic formalities.

Section 2

What Is Unattended?

CROR Definition — "Unattended"

Equipment is considered unattended when an employee is not in close enough proximity to take effective action to stop unintended movement.

The critical test is not where you are, but what you can do right now. If the equipment started moving this instant, could you stop it?

✖ Unattended

You walk 40 m to line a switch. You step into a building to grab paperwork. You are on the far end of a long cut you cannot reach quickly.

✔ Attended

You are on the controlling locomotive and can operate the air brakes. Or you are within arm's reach per CROR 109 for a specific passing train inspection.

⚠

When in doubt — secure it. There is no penalty for over-securing. The consequences of under-securing can be catastrophic.

Section 2

Understanding Grade

Railway grade is the measure of how steep a section of track is — expressed as a percentage. A 1% grade means the track rises or falls 1 metre for every 100 metres of track length.

Grade matters because gravity acts on parked equipment. The steeper the grade, the greater the force trying to roll cars downhill — and the more hand brakes are required to hold them.

📋

Grade is measured as an average over a defined distance (2+ miles for the heavy/mountain classifications).

Section 2

Grade Classifications

FLAT
0% – 1.0%

Standard rules apply. Hand brakes required per CROR 112 chart. No special Rule 66 triggers.

HEAVY GRADE
1.0% – 1.8%

Average over 2+ miles. Rule 66(e) triggers apply. Elevated securement procedures.

MOUNTAIN GRADE
>1.8%

Average over 2+ miles. Highest risk. Hand brakes go on before air recovery. Rule 66(d).

⚠

Lac-Mégantic: the parking location was a 0.92% grade — technically "flat" — yet the train ran away because the hand brake count was still insufficient for the tonnage.

Section 2

High Risk Locations

CROR Definition — High Risk Location

A track or portion of a track (other than main track, subdivision track, or siding) identified in Special Instructions, on which unattended equipment requires application of Rule 112(a).

In plain language: these are specific yard tracks or industrial spurs where the railway has determined that even a small amount of unintended movement could have serious consequences.

  • Designated in your territory's Special Instructions — check them
  • Even though it's not main track, it requires main-track-level securement
  • High risk locations are not always visually obvious — they are assigned in writing
  • Examples: tracks near public crossings, customer facilities with hazardous materials
💡

Key habit: Before securing on any non-main track, confirm with your Special Instructions whether it's a high risk location.

Section 2

Knowledge Check

? Knowledge Check

An employee steps off their locomotive and walks 25 metres to inspect a car. Is the locomotive 'attended' or 'unattended'?

Section 3

Air Brakes vs. Hand Brakes

✖ Air Brakes Alone
  • Require constant pressurized air to hold
  • Bleed off slowly when compressor stops
  • Can fail if locomotive shuts down
  • Not designed for long-term parking
✔ Hand Brakes
  • Fully mechanical — no air needed
  • Hold indefinitely when properly applied
  • Not affected by compressor failure
  • The only reliable long-term securement

At Lac-Mégantic, a locomotive fire required the engines to be shut down. When the engines stopped, the air compressors stopped. Air bled from the brake pipe. The hand brakes — only 7 of them — could not hold 10,290 tons.

△

Rule: Air brakes are for stopping and controlling a moving train. Hand brakes are for parking a stationary train. You need both — in their correct roles.

Section 3

The Vertical Wheel Hand Brake

Found on: Standard freight cars — the most common type you will encounter.

  1. Grasp the wheel

    With both hands — the chain below the wheel controls the brake rigging.

  2. Turn clockwise

    This tightens the chain and applies the brake shoes to the wheels.

  3. Continue until firm

    The wheel should strongly resist further turning. Chain must be taut.

  4. To release

    Turn counter-clockwise until fully free. Never leave partially applied.

⚠

Always confirm the chain is taut and the brake is fully engaged before recording it as an applied hand brake.

Section 3

The Stem Winder Hand Brake

Found on: Flat cars — the upright stem and horizontal wheel give it a distinctive look.

Key Features

  • Vertical stem rises from the car with a wheel on top
  • Chain runs from the wheel housing down to the brake rigging
  • Apply by turning the wheel (direction varies by manufacturer)
  • Check the car's posted brake instructions if direction is unclear
📋

When in doubt about apply direction on any hand brake — test it. Apply slightly and observe the brake rigging. If the shoes press against the wheels, you're in the right direction.

Section 3

The Ratchet Style Hand Brake

Found on: Piggyback flat cars and auto-rack cars.

How It Works

  • Uses a lever-and-pawl (ratchet) mechanism
  • Pull the lever upward to apply — the ratchet clicks into place
  • More lever strokes = more force applied
  • To release: disengage the pawl and lower the lever
🔊

The clicking sound is a feature — it confirms the ratchet is engaging. Each click means the brake is being tightened. If you don't hear clicks, check the mechanism.

Section 3

Automatic Parking Brakes

How They Engage

When brake pipe pressure drops below 20 PSI, the system automatically locks the brake cylinder piston in the extended position — retaining brake force without air pressure.

How They Release

Once brake pipe pressure recovers above 40 PSI, the locking mechanism releases automatically. No manual intervention needed.

📋

CROR Rule 112(ii): Parking brakes are considered to be hand brakes. They count toward your required hand brake total under the CROR 112(g) chart.

★

Greatest advantage: Automatic parking brakes engage during any penalty or emergency brake application without any human action. Particularly valuable in cold weather when manual application is difficult.

Section 3

The Cardinal Rule

CROR Rule 112(iii) — Cardinal Rule

The application of hand brakes must not be made while equipment is being pulled or shoved.

Why?

  • Risk of derailment: A braked wheel being dragged can climb or skip the rail
  • Brake rigging damage: Moving application creates extreme stress on linkage
  • Car separation: Sudden braking of one car while others continue can cause uncoupling
❌

Never use a hand brake as a "slow-down" device while cars are rolling. Always stop the equipment first, then apply the hand brake.

Section 3

Knowledge Check

? Knowledge Check

You are shoving 8 cars into a track. The cars are still rolling slowly. A colleague suggests applying one hand brake to help slow them. What should you do?

Section 4

Why Testing Matters

Applying a hand brake is not enough. A hand brake can be:

  • Defective or poorly maintained
  • Applied with insufficient force
  • Connected to brake rigging that has failed
  • Frozen or fouled with ice in winter

An untested hand brake is just a guess. The only way to confirm it will hold is to test it with the air brakes fully released.

🚫

The most common error: testing hand brakes while air brakes are still applied. The air does the holding — the hand brake appears to work, but it may not hold once the air bleeds off.

💡

Rule of thumb: If you haven't tested the hand brakes with air fully released, you haven't actually secured the equipment.

Section 4

Step 1: Release Air Brakes

①

First: Ensure all air brakes on the car(s) being tested are fully released before beginning the effectiveness test.

Why release air first?

If air brakes are still applied when you test, the air is doing most of the holding work. The hand brake appears sufficient — but it may not be. You need to isolate the hand brake's holding power alone.

✖ Wrong

Test with air partially applied. The hand brake appears to hold — but air is actually doing the work.

✔ Correct

Fully release all air brakes. Test only the hand brake's mechanical holding power.

Section 4

Step 2: Slack Adjustment

②

Allow slack to adjust. When slack runs in or out, it must be apparent that the hand brakes are sufficient to prevent the equipment from moving.

Slack is the small amount of play between coupled cars. When you release air brakes and slack runs in or out, there is a brief impulse of force on the hand-braked car. If the car doesn't move during this impulse, it's a good initial sign.

Observe:

  • Does the car creep forward or backward during slack action?
  • Does the brake chain become slack (indicating insufficient tension)?
  • Is the movement of each car arrested immediately?
📋

This step alone is not sufficient — it must be followed by the tractive effort test (Step 3).

Section 4

Step 3: Tractive Effort Test

③

Apply sufficient tractive effort to determine that the hand brakes prevent the equipment from moving when tractive effort is terminated.

In practice, this means:

  1. Using the locomotive, apply a controlled amount of tractive effort (gently "pull" or "push" the car)
  2. Increase effort gradually until you reach a meaningful test force
  3. Terminate the effort — release the throttle and observe
  4. If the car remains completely stationary after terminating effort, the hand brake is effective
✓

The test is pass when: after releasing tractive effort, the car does not move at all.

Section 4

Step 4: If Insufficient

④

If the effectiveness test shows the brakes are not sufficient — apply one or more additional hand brakes and re-test.

This is a loop, not a one-shot test:

⚠

There is no maximum number of times you can add and re-test. Keep going until the brakes actually hold. Your minimum from the CROR chart is just that — a minimum starting point.

Section 4

Confirming with a Colleague

CROR Rule 112(iv)

Before leaving equipment, the employee securing such equipment must confirm with another employee the manner in which it has been secured.

What this looks like in practice:

  • "I applied 6 hand brakes on track 7, cars 1 through 12. All tested and holding."
  • The colleague acknowledges and confirms understanding
  • Radio communication counts — but be specific
Why Two People?

Fatigue and distraction cause miscounts. A second person creates a safety net. This rule directly addresses the Lac-Mégantic scenario — one person, one error, 47 deaths.

What to Communicate

Location, car numbers, number of hand brakes applied, and that they have been tested for effectiveness.

Section 4

The Lead Locomotive Rule

CROR Rule 112(v)

When one or more locomotives are coupled to one or more cars and left unattended, hand brakes must be applied on all locomotives in the lead consist.

⚠

Critical detail: Hand brakes applied on locomotives do NOT count toward the number of hand brakes required on the cars. You must count them separately.

Example

Train: 2 locomotives + 50 cars. CROR chart requires 8 hand brakes for the trailing tonnage and grade.

You must apply: hand brakes on both locomotives (all lead consist locos) + 8 hand brakes on the cars. The locomotive brakes are never part of the 8.

Section 4

Knowledge Check

? Knowledge Check

You test a hand brake with air brakes released. When you apply tractive effort, the car moves slightly. What is the correct next step?

Section 5

Grade + Tonnage

Every time you leave equipment unattended, you must answer one critical question:

The Question

"How many hand brakes do I need to safely secure this equipment?"

The answer comes from two variables:

▲
Variable 1: Grade

The average grade (%) at the location where the equipment is parked. Steeper = more brakes needed.

⚖
Variable 2: Trailing Tons

The total weight of the cars (not including locomotives). Heavier = more brakes needed.

These two values are cross-referenced in the CROR Rule 112(g) hand brake chart to give you a minimum required hand brake count.

💡

Remember: Locomotive hand brakes are counted separately and are not included in the tonnage calculation.

Section 5

Reading the Chart

The CROR Rule 112(g) chart cross-references trailing tons (rows) against average grade percentage (columns) to give you the minimum number of hand brakes required.

How to use it:

  1. Find the row matching your trailing tonnage range
  2. Find the column for the average grade at your location
  3. The number at the intersection is your minimum
  4. Test your hand brakes — if insufficient, add more
⚠

The chart gives a minimum — not a target. Field conditions (weather, car condition, brake age) may require more. Always test.

Section 5

Worked Example: Lac-Mégantic

Given Information

Train weight: 10,290 trailing tons
Parking grade: 0.92%
Location: Main track, Nantes, QC

  1. Find the tonnage row

    10,290 tons falls in the row "> 10,000 – 12,000 tons"

  2. Find the grade column

    0.92% falls in the column "Average grade ≤ 1.0%"

  3. Read the intersection

    The table shows 26 hand brakes required (minimum).

Applied: 7

What the engineer actually set that night in Nantes.

Required: 26 (minimum)

What the CROR chart required for this train on this grade.

Section 5

Practice Problem 1

? Knowledge Check

A train is left unattended on a 1.2% average grade. Trailing tons: 7,000. Using the CROR 112(g) chart, how many hand brakes are required?

Section 5

Practice Problem 2

? Knowledge Check

A train stops on a 2.0% grade. Trailing tons: 15,000. The train has 2 lead locomotives. How many hand brakes are needed on the CARS (not counting locomotives)?

Section 5

Knowledge Check

? Knowledge Check

Why do locomotive hand brakes NOT count toward the chart requirement?

Section 6

Emergency Brake on Grade

When a train experiences an emergency brake application on a heavy or mountain grade, your first action is always to report.

CROR Rule 66(a)

The operating crew must immediately provide details of the situation to the proper authority, and be governed by any additional instructions received.

After reporting, your next actions depend on the grade type:

Mountain Grade (>1.8%)

Apply hand brakes immediately before attempting air recovery. See next page.

Heavy Grade (1.0–1.8%)

Check the 5 conditions (Rule 66(e)). If ANY apply → secure immediately.

Section 6

Mountain Grade Protocol

CROR Rule 66(d) — Mountain Grade

When a train experiences an emergency brake application on a mountain grade, hand brakes must be immediately applied (as per Rule 112(f)) before attempting to recover the air brake system.

There is no checklist, no exception, no discussion on a mountain grade. The sequence is:

  1. Report to proper authority

    Call RTC/dispatcher immediately with your situation.

  2. Apply hand brakes NOW

    Before touching anything related to the air system. Minimum count from the CROR 112(g) chart.

  3. Attempt air recovery

    Only after hand brakes are applied and equipment is secured.

🚫

The order is non-negotiable on mountain grade: Hand brakes first. Air recovery second. Never the reverse.

Section 6

Heavy Grade: 5 Conditions

📋

CROR Rule 66(e): On a heavy grade emergency, secure immediately if ANY of the following conditions exist:

  • 1
    Ambient temperature is −20°C or colder
  • 2
    Temperature is −15 to −19°C AND snow is 3+ inches above top of rail
  • 3
    Crew has experienced unusual braking conditions or difficulty controlling speed
  • 4
    Doubt exists about the ability to safely recover and control the movement
  • 5
    More than one emergency brake application has occurred on the grade
⚠

If NONE of the 5 conditions apply → attempt recovery. If recovery fails → secure immediately. See next page.

Section 6

Recovery Attempt Procedure

On a heavy grade where none of the 5 conditions apply:

  1. Attempt air recovery

    Try to recover from the emergency brake application normally.

  2. If air recovers

    Inspect for cause. Continue with caution and inform proper authority.

  3. If air does NOT recover

    Immediately inspect the train for the cause of the failure.

  4. If cause cannot be found or corrected

    Secure the train immediately using hand brakes per Rule 112.

⚠

You cannot simply wait indefinitely for air to recover. If you cannot fix it quickly, you must secure. There is no "wait and see" option on a grade.

📋

Throughout this process, the proper authority must be kept informed. Decisions about movement or recovery may require their approval.

Section 6

The Entire Train Rule

CROR Rule 66(b) — The Entire Train Rule

When a train experiences an emergency brake application and any portion of the train is located on a mountain grade, the entire train must be considered to be on mountain grade.

✖ Wrong Thinking

"Only the rear 10 cars are on the mountain grade. The rest of the train is on flat track. I'll apply mountain grade rules only to those cars."

✔ Correct Approach

ANY portion on mountain grade = entire train treated as mountain grade. Apply mountain grade protocol to the whole consist, no exceptions.

Section 6

Derailment & Separation

CROR Rule 66(c)

In the event of a derailment or a train separation on heavy or mountain grade, the portion of the train at greatest risk of unintended movement must be secured first.

Determining "greatest risk" requires judgement:

  • Which portion is on the steepest section of grade?
  • Which portion is heavier?
  • Which portion, if it moves, threatens infrastructure or populated areas?
  • Which portion has fewer hand brakes already applied?
⚠

If possible, secure both portions simultaneously using available crew. If you must choose — greatest risk first, always.

Section 6

Knowledge Check

? Knowledge Check

Your train has an emergency brake application. You determine that 15 cars at the rear are on a 2.1% grade. The rest of the train is on flat track. What grade protocol applies to the ENTIRE train?

Section 7

When NOT Unattended

Equipment is NOT considered unattended (and does not require securement) in only two situations:

Option A

A qualified employee is on the controlling locomotive and is able to operate the air brake system.

Option B (Specific)

A locomotive engineer is on the ground per CROR 109, within arm's reach of the locomotive, specifically for passing train or transfer inspections.

⚠

Option B is very narrow — it applies only to specific inspection scenarios. It is not a general "I'm nearby" exception. If neither condition applies, equipment is unattended and must be secured.

Section 7

Main Track Requirements

On main track and high risk locations, the highest standard of securement applies. Equipment is only considered secured if it meets one of two conditions:

Condition A — Air Connected

Coupled to a controlling locomotive, brake pipe open, AND a qualified employee able to operate air brakes.

Condition B — No Air Source

A sufficient number of hand brakes applied per Rule 112(g) chart, plus any additional securement required by field conditions.

📋

Additional securement may be required on top of the chart minimum if: weather conditions are severe, grade is steep, or equipment condition warrants it.

Section 7

Yard Tracks: 5 Methods

When equipment is left unattended in a yard track, at least one of the following securement methods must be used:

1. Hand Brakes

Minimum per CROR 112(g) chart. Must be tested for effectiveness.

2. Bowled Terrain

Track configured to prevent unintended outward movement of equipment.

3. Retarders

Mechanical devices in the track structure that resist wheel movement.

4. Wheel Chocks or Skates

Physical blocks placed against wheels. Prevent rolling in the blocked direction.

5. Air Brakes (up to 2 hrs)

Only without an air source and only when all 4 special conditions are met. Time-limited. See next page.

Section 7

The 2-Hour Air Brake Rule

Air brakes without a connected air source may be used as the sole securement in a yard track for up to 2 hours — but only when ALL four conditions are met:

  1. 10 or more cars

    The method only applies to cuts of 10+ cars.

  2. Brake pipe fully vented

    Vented at a service rate OR an emergency application has occurred.

  3. System sufficiently charged

    Enough air pressure to ensure proper brake application throughout.

  4. Angle cock fully open (freight)

    On freight equipment, the angle cock at the end of the cut must be left fully open.

⌛

After 2 hours — regardless of condition — you must revert to hand brakes, bowled terrain, retarders, or wheel chocks.

Section 7

Weather & Conditions

⛈

Rule 112 requirement: Exceptional weather conditions such as high winds must be considered and may require additional securement beyond the CROR chart minimum.

ConditionImpactAction
High windsIncreased lateral/longitudinal force on equipmentAdd hand brakes beyond minimum
Extreme coldAir leaks faster; brake cylinders may freezeDo not rely on air; add hand brakes
Ice / snowReduced wheel-rail friction; brake rigging may freezeTest extra carefully; add brakes
Wet railReduced friction on gradeConsider additional brakes
💡

When in doubt: Add more hand brakes. You will never be criticized for over-securing. You may face serious consequences for under-securing.

Section 7

Knowledge Check

? Knowledge Check

You are leaving 12 cars in a yard track. No air source is available. It is 3:00 PM. What is the maximum time you can use air brakes as the sole securement method (assuming all 4 conditions are met)?

Section 8

Care & Control

CROR Rule 112 — Switching

While switching, equipment must be under your care and control. If you will engage in an activity that diminishes your ability to effectively and safely stop equipment — consider it unattended and secure accordingly.

Apply the same test as always: "If this equipment started moving right now, could I take effective action to stop it?"

  • If you leave the cab to line a switch → effective action possible?
  • If you step inside a building → effective action possible?
  • If you kick cars to the far end of a long track → effective action possible?
⚠

Switching does not grant you a blanket exception from securement rules. The test is always about capability to act — not about intent or activity type.

Section 8

Remaining Equipment

Rule

When required to move a portion of equipment from a track, ensure any remaining equipment is properly secured before you leave.

When cutting off from a train, the following applies to the remaining cars:

  • Close the angle cock on the remaining equipment properly — not quickly, not carelessly
  • Air brakes must be applied in emergency or full service on remaining cars
  • The angle cock on remaining equipment must be left fully open
  • If the remaining equipment is unattended → apply the required number of hand brakes and test them
⚠

The moment you cut off and move the locomotive, the cars you leave behind are unattended equipment. All securement rules apply immediately.

Section 8

Cutting Off in Motion

When cars will be cut off in motion and controlled by hand brakes:

Rule 112

Hand brakes used to control cars cut off in motion must be tested in advance to confirm they are operational before the equipment is cut off.

⚠

Do NOT allow cars to roll onto standing equipment unless it is known that the brakes on the standing equipment are sufficient to prevent unintended movement of all equipment after coupling.

Why Pre-Test?

Once the cut is released, there's no opportunity to correct a defective hand brake. If it fails during a flying kick, you have a runaway. Test before the cut — not after.

Section 8

Knowledge Check

? Knowledge Check

You kick 8 cars onto a track where 5 cars are already sitting. Before allowing the rolling cars to reach the standing cars, what must you know?

Wrap-Up

The 5 Golden Rules

These five rules summarize everything in this module. Know them. Apply them every time.

1

If you can't take effective action → it's unattended → secure it.
The test is capability, not location.

2

Hand brake count = CROR 112(g) chart (grade × trailing tons).
Never estimate. Always look it up.

3

Test every hand brake with air brakes fully released.
An untested hand brake is a guess.

4

Mountain grade: hand brakes first, then recover air.
Rule 66(d) — no exceptions, no discussion.

5

Confirm securement with a colleague every time.
Two people means two safety nets.

Wrap-Up

Final Scenario

? Knowledge Check

It is −17°C. Snow is 4 inches above the top of rail. Your 11,500-ton train has an emergency brake application on a 1.5% average grade (2.3-mile section). What must you do FIRST after calling the proper authority?

Wrap-Up

Pre-Exam Instructions

📝

The pre-exam is NOT for marks. It is a learning tool. Research shows that being tested immediately after instruction dramatically improves long-term retention (Make It Stick, Brown et al., 2014).

Instructions

  • Work with your team — discuss every answer together
  • You may use written materials to find answers
  • No cell phones, computers, or AI during the exam
  • Time limit: 20 minutes
  • When finished, hand your exam to the instructor
  • You will be given another team's exam to mark
🏅

You are ready. You have covered every rule, every procedure, and every key concept. Trust your training.

Wrap-Up

References

SourceDescription
CROR Rule 112Securement of Unattended Equipment — full rule and 112(g) chart. Transport Canada.
